The **Cliff House** is a [neo-classical](/wiki/Neo-classical_architecture "Neo-classical architecture") style building perched on the headland above the cliffs just north of [Ocean Beach](/wiki/Ocean_Beach_(San_Francisco) "Ocean Beach (San Francisco)"). Located on the [West Side](/wiki/West_Side_(San_Francisco) "West Side (San Francisco)") of [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco") in the city's [Outer Richmond](/wiki/Outer_Richmond,_San_Francisco "Outer Richmond, San Francisco") neighborhood, the building overlooks the site of the [Sutro Baths](/wiki/Sutro_Baths "Sutro Baths") ruins, [Seal Rocks](/wiki/Seal_Rocks_(San_Francisco,_California) "Seal Rocks (San Francisco, California)"), and is part of the [Golden Gate National Recreation Area](/wiki/Golden_Gate_National_Recreation_Area "Golden Gate National Recreation Area") and operated by the [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service") (NPS). The Cliff House is owned by the NPS; the building's terrace hosts a room-sized [camera obscura](/wiki/Camera_obscura "Camera obscura").

For most of the Cliff House's history, since 1863, the building's main draw has been restaurants and bars where patrons could enjoy the Pacific Ocean views. Since 1977, these restaurants and bars have been run by a private operator under contract with the National Park Service. In December 2020, the 47-year operator of these amenities announced that it was closing, and it criticized the NPS for not having signed a new long-term lease with any operator since its own prior 20-year lease had expired in June 2018.[[1]](#cite_note-Gafni,_Matthias-2020-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Staff-2020-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Guardian-3)

Dozens of ships have run aground on the southern shore of the Golden Gate below the Cliff House.[[4]](#cite_note-Delgado2009-4)

## First Cliff House (1863–1894)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cliff_House,_San_Francisco&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: First Cliff House (1863–1894)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Sea_Lion_Rock%2C_the_Cliff_House%2C_San_Francisco_LACMA_M.91.359.74.jpg/250px-Sea_Lion_Rock%2C_the_Cliff_House%2C_San_Francisco_LACMA_M.91.359.74.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sea_Lion_Rock,_the_Cliff_House,_San_Francisco_LACMA_M.91.359.74.jpg)

First Cliff House, c. 1868

[![James Malcolm Wilkins, proprietor](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/James_Malcolm_Wilkins.png/250px-James_Malcolm_Wilkins.png)](/wiki/File:James_Malcolm_Wilkins.png)

James Malcolm Wilkins, proprietor

Anecdotal stories claim that in 1858 [Samuel Brannan](/wiki/Samuel_Brannan "Samuel Brannan") paid $1,500 for lumber salvaged from a ship that foundered on the rocky shore's basalt cliffs near Seal Rocks[[5]](#cite_note-5) and built the first Cliff House. While Brannan may have constructed a building there, no historical evidence of this building exists and its role in the origin of the Cliff House remains [apocryphal](/wiki/Apocryphal "Apocryphal").[[6]](#cite_note-6)
The Cliff House was built by Senator John Buckley and C. C. Butler, opened in 1863 and leased to Captain Junius G. Foster.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-Call-9) It was a long trek on foot from the city and the restaurant hosted mostly horseback riders, small-game hunters or picnickers on day outings. With the opening of the privately built Point Lobos toll road a year later, the Cliff House became a Sunday destination among the "[carriage trade](/wiki/Carriage_trade_(social_class) "Carriage trade (social class)")". Later the builders of the toll road constructed a two-mile speedway adjacent to it where well-to-do San Franciscans raced their horses along the way. On weekends, there was little room at the Cliff House hitching racks for tethering the horses for the thousands of rigs. Soon, [omnibus](/wiki/Wagonette "Wagonette"), railways and [streetcar](/wiki/Streetcar "Streetcar") lines made it to near [Lone Mountain](/wiki/Lone_Mountain_(California) "Lone Mountain (California)") where passengers transferred to stagecoach lines to the beach. The growth of [Golden Gate Park](/wiki/Golden_Gate_Park "Golden Gate Park") attracted beach travelers, in search of meals and a look at the [sea lions](/wiki/Sea_Lions "Sea Lions") sunning themselves on [Seal Rocks](/wiki/Seal_Rocks_(San_Francisco,_California) "Seal Rocks (San Francisco, California)") just off the cliffs, to visit the area. In 1877, the toll road, now Geary Street, was purchased by the city for approximately $25,000.

In 1883, after a few years of downturn, the Cliff House was bought by [Adolph Sutro](/wiki/Adolph_Sutro "Adolph Sutro"), who had made a fortune in silver by solving the problems of ventilating and draining the mines of Nevada's [Comstock Lode](/wiki/Comstock_Lode "Comstock Lode"). After a few years of quiet management by James M. Wilkins, the Cliff House was severely damaged when the schooner *Parallel*, abandoned with burning oil lamps and a cargo including dynamite powder, exploded while aground at Lands End early in the morning of January 16, 1887. The blast was heard a hundred miles away[[10]](#cite_note-disasters-10) and demolished the entire north wing of the tavern. The building was repaired, but was later completely destroyed by fire on Christmas night 1894 due to a defective [flue](/wiki/Flue "Flue").[[9]](#cite_note-Call-9)[[11]](#cite_note-11) Wilkins was unable to save the guest register, which included the signatures of three U.S. Presidents and dozens of world-famous visitors. This incarnation of the Cliff House, with its various extensions, had lasted for 31 years.

## Second Cliff House (1896–1907)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cliff_House,_San_Francisco&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Second Cliff House (1896–1907)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/San_Francisco%27s_Cliff_House_Restaurant_and_Seal_Rocks%2C_ca.1900_%28CHS-4756%29_crop.jpg/330px-San_Francisco%27s_Cliff_House_Restaurant_and_Seal_Rocks%2C_ca.1900_%28CHS-4756%29_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Francisco%27s_Cliff_House_Restaurant_and_Seal_Rocks,_ca.1900_(CHS-4756)_crop.jpg)

Second Cliff House, c. 1900

In 1896, Adolph Sutro rebuilt the Cliff House from the ground up as a seven-story Victorian chateau, called by some "the Gingerbread Palace", below his estate on the bluffs of [Sutro Heights](/wiki/Sutro_Heights_Park "Sutro Heights Park"). This was the same year work began on the [Sutro Baths](/wiki/Sutro_Baths "Sutro Baths") in a small cove immediately north of the restaurant. The baths included six of the large indoor swimming pools, a museum, a skating rink and other pleasure grounds. Great throngs of San Franciscans arrived on steam trains, bicycles, carts and horse wagons on Sunday excursions. Sutro purchased some of the collection of stuffed animals, artwork, and historic items from [Woodward's Gardens](/wiki/Woodward%27s_Gardens "Woodward's Gardens") to display at both the Cliff House and Sutro Baths.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The 1896 Cliff House survived the [1906 earthquake](/wiki/1906_San_Francisco_earthquake "1906 San Francisco earthquake") with little damage, but burned to the ground on the evening of September 7, 1907.[[10]](#cite_note-disasters-10)

The first section of cable for the [Commercial Pacific Cable Company](/wiki/Commercial_Pacific_Cable_Company "Commercial Pacific Cable Company") was laid in 1902 by the cable ship CS Silvertown from [Ocean Beach](/wiki/Ocean_Beach,_San_Francisco "Ocean Beach, San Francisco"), adjacent to the famous Cliff House in San Francisco to Honolulu.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Third Cliff House (1909–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cliff_House,_San_Francisco&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Third Cliff House (1909–present)")]

### 1909–1937

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cliff_House,_San_Francisco&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1909–1937")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/San_Francisco_--_The_New_Cliff_House._On_the_Road_of_a_Thousand_Wonders_%28pcard-print-pub-pc-71a%29.jpg/330px-San_Francisco_--_The_New_Cliff_House._On_the_Road_of_a_Thousand_Wonders_%28pcard-print-pub-pc-71a%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Francisco_--_The_New_Cliff_House._On_the_Road_of_a_Thousand_Wonders_(pcard-print-pub-pc-71a).jpg)

Third Cliff House, c. 1909

After the fire, Emma Merritt, Sutro's daughter, commissioned [Reid & Reid](/wiki/Reid_%26_Reid "Reid & Reid") to rebuild the restaurant in a neo-classical style. It was completed within two years and is the basis of the structure seen today. In 1914, the guidebook *Bohemian San Francisco* described it as "one of the great Bohemian restaurants of San Francisco. ... while you have thought you had good breakfasts before this, you know that now you are having the best of them all."[[14]](#cite_note-14)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Cliff_House_Comp.jpg/330px-Cliff_House_Comp.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cliff_House_Comp.jpg)

Top: Third Cliff House, c. 1950, following significant additions and modifications in 1949.   
Bottom: Third Cliff House, 2009, following 2004 renovation to original 1909 aesthetic

### 1937–2003

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cliff_House,_San_Francisco&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1937–2003")]

In 1937, George and Leo Whitney purchased the Cliff House, to complement their [Playland-at-the-Beach](/wiki/Playland_(San_Francisco) "Playland (San Francisco)") attraction nearby, and extensively remodelled it into an American [roadhouse](/wiki/Roadhouse_(facility) "Roadhouse (facility)"). From 1955 to 1966, a "[Sky Tram](/wiki/Aerial_tramway "Aerial tramway")" operated across the Sutro Baths basin, taking up to 25 visitors at a time from Point Lobos, enhanced by an artificial waterfall, to the outer balcony of the Cliff House.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

In 1972, upon the closing of [Playland](/wiki/Playland_(San_Francisco) "Playland (San Francisco)"), the [Musée Mécanique](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_M%C3%A9canique "Musée Mécanique"), a museum of 20th-century [penny arcade](/wiki/Penny_arcade "Penny arcade") games, was moved into the basement of the Cliff House.[[16]](#cite_note-Preservation1-16) In the early 1970s the land-side exterior of the building was decorated with an expansive mural painting depicting crashing waves, painted by artist-musicians (and future members of San Francisco rock band [The Tubes](/wiki/The_Tubes "The Tubes")) Michael Cotten and [Prairie Prince](/wiki/Prairie_Prince "Prairie Prince").

The building was acquired by the [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service") (NPS) in 1977 and became part of the [Golden Gate National Recreation Area](/wiki/Golden_Gate_National_Recreation_Area "Golden Gate National Recreation Area"). In connection with this acquisition, the NPS contracted with Dan and Mary Hountalas as official concessionaires of the property. The NPS renewed its contract with the Hountalas family in 1998, through the family's company, Peanut Wagon, Inc.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### 2003–2020

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cliff_House,_San_Francisco&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2003–2020")]

In 2003, as part of an extensive renovation, many of Whitney's additions were removed and the building was restored to its 1909 appearance. A new two-story wing was constructed overlooking what were by then the ruins of the Sutro Baths. (The Baths burned to the ground on June 26, 1966.[[10]](#cite_note-disasters-10)) During the site restoration, the [Musée Mécanique](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_M%C3%A9canique "Musée Mécanique") was moved to [Fisherman's Wharf](/wiki/Fisherman%27s_Wharf,_San_Francisco "Fisherman's Wharf, San Francisco").[[16]](#cite_note-Preservation1-16)

The Cliff House had two restaurants, the casual dining Bistro Restaurant and the more formal Sutro's. Additionally, the Terrace Room served a Sunday brunch buffet. There was a gift shop in the building, and the historic [camera obscura](/wiki/Camera_Obscura_(San_Francisco,_California) "Camera Obscura (San Francisco, California)") is on a deck overlooking the ocean. Peanut Wagon continued to manage Cliff House operations and worked with the Park Service during the extensive site restoration that was completed in 2004.

During the [2013 government shutdown](/wiki/United_States_federal_government_shutdown_of_2013 "United States federal government shutdown of 2013"), October 1–17, the US Park Service ordered the restaurant closed. The owners defied the order, but were forced to close. They reopened with permission on October 12, 2013.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### 2021 closure and future prospects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cliff_House,_San_Francisco&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2021 closure and future prospects")]

The concessionaires of the Cliff House reported on December 13, 2020, that they would be closing their doors on December 31, 2020. They blamed losses from the closure due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_States "COVID-19 pandemic in the United States"), and their landlord, the National Park Service (NPS), for delaying a long term-lease; the restaurant had been operating under a series of short-term leases since June 2018.[[1]](#cite_note-Gafni,_Matthias-2020-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Staff-2020-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Guardian-3) According to the National Park Service's website, a 3.5-year lease had been offered to the vendor (the Hountalas family doing business as Peanut Wagon Inc.) on December 30, which was turned down. On December 31, 2020, the Cliff House's sign was removed.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

The NPS says that it "is committed to maintaining this iconic building", but that the "solicitation process [for a new vendor] for this operation is currently paused as a result of the pandemic."[[20]](#cite_note-20) On February 2, 2021, the [San Francisco Board of Supervisors](/wiki/San_Francisco_Board_of_Supervisors "San Francisco Board of Supervisors") passed a resolution urging the NPS to find an immediate vendor for the restaurant while it searched for a long-term tenant. The Park Service confirmed that they planned to do so.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

#### Trademark issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cliff_House,_San_Francisco&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Trademark issues")]

In the wake of the departure of the Hountalas family and its company as the private concessionaire for the Cliff House, it emerged that the company had secured certain "Cliff House"-related trademarks. This led news organizations to speculate as to whether a future concessionaire would be able to use the "Cliff House" name to protect and promote the identity of the institution.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cliff_House,_San_Francisco&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Willard_Worden%2C_Storm_on_Ocean_Beach%2C_showing_Cliff_House_and_Seal_Rocks_1904.jpg/250px-Willard_Worden%2C_Storm_on_Ocean_Beach%2C_showing_Cliff_House_and_Seal_Rocks_1904.jpg)](/wiki/File:Willard_Worden,_Storm_on_Ocean_Beach,_showing_Cliff_House_and_Seal_Rocks_1904.jpg)

[Willard Worden](/wiki/Willard_Worden "Willard Worden"), *Storm on Ocean Beach*, gelatin silver print, 1904.

* The area immediately around the Cliff House is part of the setting of [Jack London](/wiki/Jack_London "Jack London")'s novel *[The Scarlet Plague](/wiki/The_Scarlet_Plague "The Scarlet Plague")* (1912).[[24]](#cite_note-24)
* Jack London also sets the meeting of Maud Sangster and Pat Glendon Jr. here in *[The Abysmal Brute](/wiki/The_Abysmal_Brute "The Abysmal Brute")* (1913).
* Wallace Stegner refers to the Cliff House in his Pulitzer Prize-winning novel *[Angle of Repose](/wiki/Angle_of_Repose "Angle of Repose")* in a letter from Susan Ward to her friend Augusta, in which she describes a visit to San Francisco in 1867 (1971).[[25]](#cite_note-25)
* An image of the second Cliff House was used on the cover of the album *[Imaginos](/wiki/Imaginos "Imaginos")* by [Blue Öyster Cult](/wiki/Blue_%C3%96yster_Cult "Blue Öyster Cult").
* The Cliff House appears in a scene in the 1948 film *[Race Street](/wiki/Race_Street_(film) "Race Street (film)")*, starring [George Raft](/wiki/George_Raft "George Raft").
* The Cliff House is featured in the [Ubisoft](/wiki/Ubisoft "Ubisoft") video game *[Watch Dogs 2](/wiki/Watch_Dogs_2 "Watch Dogs 2")*.
* The Cliff House appears briefly in the climax of the 1958 film *[The Lineup](/wiki/The_Lineup_(film) "The Lineup (film)")*, at [Sutro Baths](/wiki/Sutro_Baths "Sutro Baths"), starring [Eli Wallach](/wiki/Eli_Wallach "Eli Wallach") and directed by [Don Siegel](/wiki/Don_Siegel "Don Siegel").
* Robert Frost references the Cliff House in his poem "A Record Stride" (*A Further Range*, 1936).
* Scenes were shot at the third Cliff house for the 1924 film [Greed](/wiki/Greed_(1924_film) "Greed (1924 film)")
